authors: Tania Babina

title: Risky Insurance: Life-Cycle Insurance Portfolio Choice with Incomplete Markets
authors: Joseph S. Briggs, Ciaran Rogers, Christopher Tonetti
summary: We study consumer demand for savings, life insurance, annuities, and long-term care insurance using novel survey data and a structural life-cycle model. We document that individuals perceive substantial insurance nonpayment risk, and these beliefs predict ownership. title: Comparative Advantage and Colonial Monopoly: The Political Economy of Exclusive Trading Companies
authors: Sebastian Galiani, Ivan Lopez Cruz, Alessandra A. Palazzo, Gustavo Torrens
summary: This paper analyzes a central colonial institution: the monopolization of foreign trade through exclusive trading companies. title: Hysteresis and Selection in the Rise of Fascism: The ‘Ordinary Men’ of the Nazi Party
authors: Luis Bosshart, Max Deter, Leander Heldring, Cathrin C. Mohr, Matthias Weigand
summary: We digitize and analyze the near-universe of National Socialist German Workers’ Party (NSDAP) membership records and link them to population and industrial censuses. Four findings emerge. title: How Reform Happens
authors: Simeon Djankov, Edward L. Glaeser, Andrei Shleifer
summary: What determines whether and how regulations are reformed? We use a newly constructed data set of 3,590 successful and failed regulatory reforms in 189 countries, between 2005 and 2022, to address this question. title: Gender-Specific Economic Shocks and Household Bargaining Power
authors: Rania Gihleb, Osea Giuntella, Dor Morag
summary: Using machine learning and product-level data on single men’s and single women’s consumption patterns, we develop an index that quantifies the “gendered” nature of consumer goods. title: Does Employment Slow Cognitive Decline? Evidence from Labor Market Shocks
authors: Noah Arman Kouchekinia, David Neumark, Tim A. Bruckner
summary: With large gains in life expectancy, the population share of disability due to cognitive decline and dementia has substantially increased. Many older adults in the United States leave the workforce well before age 65. title: Hedging Ambiguity with Pro-Social Preferences: an Illustration from Green Finance
authors: Geoffrey Heal, Marcella Lucchetta
summary: We explore how pro-social preferences interact with asymmetric ambiguity to influence investment decisions. We develop a model where financial returns are ambiguous (e.g., due to policy uncertainties), while social impact returns are risky or less ambiguous. title: On the Resilience of Payment Methods
authors: Fernando E. Alvarez, David Argente, Diana Van Patten
summary: We study the resilience of payment systems to large disruptions in digital infrastructure caused by natural disasters and outages. title: Intermediate Input Prices and the Labor Share
authors: Juanma Castro-Vincenzi, Benny Kleinman
summary: We argue that the relative price of materials is an important determinant of the labor share of income. summary: Can active choice mitigate the effects of preset defaults? We study this question using a difference-in-differences design around the rollout of the EU’s Digital Markets Act, which required iOS and Android to display browser choice screens under certain conditions. title: The Welfare Effects of Protecting Older Workers
authors: Todd Morris, Stefan Staubli, Benoit Dostie
summary: We evaluate the welfare effects of five provincial mandatory retirement bans in Canada from 2005 to 2009 using linked employer-employee tax data. The bans sharply reduce retirements at age 65, with sizable announcement effects and heterogeneity across industries. title: How (un)Stable Are LLM Occupational Exposure Scores? Evidence from Multi-Model Replication
authors: Michelle Yin, Hoa Vu, Claudia Persico
summary: A rapidly growing literature estimates AI's labor-market effects using large language models (LLMs) to self-assess occupational exposure. We demonstrate these measures are highly fragile. title: Early Childcare Attendance and Cognitive skills in Adolescence
authors: Ingvild Almås, Nina Drange, Costas Meghir, Henrik D. Zachrisson
summary: This paper examines the impact of early childcare on academic achievement for children in grade 5 and grade 9, based on a 2003 policy expansion that created quasi-random variation in slot availability for children aged 1–2. title: Effects of Expanding Contraceptive Choice: New Evidence from Virginia's Contraceptive Access Initiative
authors: Jessica H. Kiser, Analisa Packham, Janelle Anthony, Evelyn Escobar, Emily Yeatts
summary: In 2018, the Virginia Department of Health implemented the Contraceptive Access Initiative (CAI) to increase access to long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs). title: The Domestic Political Economy of War: Evidence from Russia
authors: Alena Gorbuntsova, Gaurav Khanna, Sultan Mehmood
summary: Wars are often framed as responses to external threats or shifts in the regional balance of power. Yet they can also serve domestic political ends. title: China's Global Ownership
authors: Jennie Bai, Luc Laeven, Yaojun Ke, Hong Ru
summary: We study the global footprint and real effects of Chinese overseas corporate ownership. summary: Spain, with one of the highest life expectancies globally and a rapidly ageing population, faces growing challenges in sustaining its pension, healthcare, and long-term care systems. title: Market Power in Mortgage Pricing: the Role of Referral Lending
authors: Dayin Zhang, Panle Jia Barwick, Lu Han, Jonathan Kroah
summary: Despite intense competition among mortgage lenders, borrowers face substantial price dispersion.
